content_html:
MGT10009 Contemporary Management Principles
Lecture 10b The adaptive Org
Dr David Qian dqian@swin.edu.au
Peter Moran pmoran@swin.edu.au

Learning Objectives
9-1	Discuss why it is critical for organizations to be responsive.
9-2 Describe the qualities of an organic organization structure.
9-3 Explain how a firm can be both big and small.
9-4 Summarize how firms organize to meet customer requirements.
9-5 Identify ways that firms organize around different types of technology.
‹#›

Management in Action Netflix’s Agile Culture
Netflix has changed from a D V D rental company to becoming a leader in digital streaming. It is now producing TV shows and movies. This agile approach is supported by a fluid approach to management in contrast to a hierarchical chain of command and an open communication system in which information is shared at a levels of the organization.
This has been a winning approach as Netflix has more than 160 million subscribers.
‹#›

The Responsive Organization
Mechanistic organization:
A form of organization that seeks to maximize internal efficiency.
Organic structure:
An organizational form that emphasizes flexibility.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.1 Formal and Informal Organization Structures
SOURCE: Adapted from G. Soda and A. Zaheer, “A Network Perspective on Organizational Architecture: Performance Effects of the Interplay of Formal and Informal Organization,” Strategic Management Journal 33 (2012), pp. 751–71.
‹#›

Organizing around Core Capabilities
Core capability:
The knowledge, expertise, or skill that underlies a company’s ability to be a leader in providing a range of goods or services.
Ordinary capabilities:
Basic administrative and operational functions needed.
Dynamic capabilities:
"Higher-level" strategic capabilities that aid rapid adaptation.
‹#›

Managing Resources for Competitive Advantage
Accumulate the right resources.
Combine resources to produce capabilities.
Leverage resources.
‹#›

Strategic Alliances
Formal relationship created among independent organizations with the purpose of joint pursuit of mutual goals
‹#›

Management in Action When Agility Is a Matter of Life and Death
When COVID-19 began sweeping across the United States in 2020, 3M worked to quickly increase the supply of N-95 masks. They added more manufacturing lines and automated equipment to increase production by 5 times as it had at the start of the year. Their approach included using strategic alliances.
In what ways did 3M use its ordinary and dynamic core capabilities to address the COVID-19 crisis?
Given the unique challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic, which types of strategic alliances do you think will be most helpful for 3M to utilize and why?
‹#›

Exhibit 9.2 How I’s Can Become We’s
The best alliances are true partnerships that meet these criteria:
Relationship: Both partners work hard on developing a positive working relationship.
Metrics: The partners measure how they are going to succeed, not just the end result.
Differences: The partners embrace differences in one another.
Control: Both partners encourage collaborative behavior with less emphasis on formal systems and structures.
Management: The partners manage their internal stakeholders who are involved in the alliance.
SOURCE: Adapted from J. Hughes and J. Weiss, “Simple Rules for Making Alliances Work,” Harvard Business Review, November 2007, www.hbr.org.
‹#›

Decentralization
Delegating responsibility and authority decentralizes decision making.
Centralized organizations are where high-level executives make most decisions and pass them down to lower levels for implementation.
Decentralized organizations are where lower-level managers make important decisions.
Sometimes organizations change their degree of centralization, depending on the particular challenges they face.
‹#›

The Case for Big and Small Organizations
Big.
Larger size helps create economies of scale.
Larger size helps develop economies of scope.
Small.
Large organizations can have difficulty managing relationships.
Large organizations are more difficult to coordinate and control.
Nimble small firms may outmaneuver big bureaucracies.
‹#›

SPOTLIGHT ON… Scaling and Increasing the Impact of Social Enterprises.
Social enterprises typically begin as small, grassroots efforts whose goal is to create a sustainable-organization that helps solve important social problems.
As social enterprises try to become larger, what unique challenges do they face?
What are some potential drawbacks of partnering with governments or big businesses?
‹#›

Downsizing
The planned elimination of positions or jobs
Eliminate functions, hierarchical levels, or entire units.
Replace full-time employees with part-time or temporary workers.
‹#›

Customers and the Responsive Organization
Managers must stay focused on at least three core players known as the strategic triangle:
The company itself.
The competition.
The customer.
‹#›

Customer Relationship Management
Customer relationship management (C R M):
A multifaceted process focusing on creating two-way exchanges with customers to foster intimate knowledge of their needs, wants, and buying patterns.
Value chain:
The sequence of activities that flow from raw materials to the delivery of a good or service, with additional value created at each step.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.3 Generic Value Chain
SOURCE: M. Porter, Competitive Advantage: Creating and Sustaining Superior Performance (New York: Free Press, 19 85).
‹#›

Spotlight on… Social CRM
Salesforce’s Service Cloud offers a solution used by companies including Adidas, The Container Store, and Athena Health. Service Cloud software scans for and locates messages about clients’ products on social media platforms. When the software detects a problem, Service Cloud agents then step in and offer to help, potentially saving its client’s relationship with a customer.
Given how often things go viral, how consequential do you think any single viral thread is for an organization today?
Do you think CRM offers a solution agile enough to keep up with the speed of e-commerce and social media? Why or why not?
‹#›

Quality Initiatives
Total quality management (T Q M)
An integrative approach to management that supports the attainment of customer satisfaction through a wide variety of tools and techniques that result in high-quality goods and services.
Six sigma quality:
Systematically analyzing work processes to identify and eliminate virtually all causes of defects, standardizing the processes to reach the lowest practicable level of any cause of customer dissatisfaction.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.4 Deming’s 14 Points of Quality
Deming’s 14 Points of Quality
Create constancy of purpose.
Adopt the new philosophy.
Cease dependence on mass inspection.
End practice of awarding business on price tag alone.
Improve constantly and forever the system of production and service.
Institute training and retraining.
Institute leadership.
Drive our fear.
Break down barriers among departments.
Eliminate slogans, exhortations, and arbitrary targets.
Eliminate numerical quotas.
Remove barriers to pride in workmanship.
Institute a vigorous program of education and retraining.
Take action to accomplish the transformation.
‹#›

I S O 9001
A series of quality standards developed by the International Organization for Standardization to improve total quality in all businesses for the benefit of producers and consumers
Seven principles:
Customer focus.
Leadership.
Involvement of people.
Process approach.
Improvement.
Evidence-based decision making.
Relationship management.
‹#›

Technology Configurations
Small batch:
Technologies that produce goods and services in low volume.
Large batch:
Technologies that produce goods and services in high volume.
Continuous process:
A process that is highly automated and has a continuous production flow.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.5 Key Features in Mass Customization
Area
High Variety and Customization
Product design
Collaborative design; significant input from customers
Short product development cycles and Constant innovation
Operations and processes
Flexible processes
Business process reengineering (B P R)
Use of modules
Continuous improvement (C I)
Reduced setup and changeover times
Reduced lead times and production to order and shorter cycle times
JIT delivery and processing of materials and componentsProduction to order and shorter cycle times
Quality management
Quality measured in customer delight
Defects treated as capability failures
Organizational structure
Dynamic network of relatively autonomous operating units
Learning relationships and team-based structure
Integration of the value chain
Workforce management
Empowerment of employees and broad job descriptions
High value: knowledge, information, diversity of employee capabilities
New product teams
Emphasis
Low-cost production of high-quality, customized products
SOURCE: Reprinted with permission of APICS—The Educational Society for Resource Management, Production and Inventory Management 41, no. 1 (2000), pp. 56–65.
‹#›

Manufacturing
Computer-integrated manufacturing (C I M):
The use of computer-aided design and computer-aided manufacturing to sequence and optimize multiple production processes.
Flexible factories:
Manufacturing plants that have short production runs, are organized around products, and use decentralized scheduling.
Lean manufacturing:
Operations that eliminate unnecessary steps in the production process and continually striving for improvement.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.6 Conditions that Support Lean Manufacturing
Conditions
People are broadly trained rather than specialized.
Communication is informal and horizontal among line workers.
Equipment is general-purpose.
Work is organized in teams, or cells, that produce a group of similar products.
Supplier relationships are long-term and cooperative.
Product development is concurrent, not sequential, and is done by cross-functional teams.
SOURCES: F. Sahin, “Manufacturing Competitiveness: Different Systems to Achieve the Same Results,” Production and Inventory Management Journal 41, no. 1 (First Quarter 2000), pp. 56–65; G. S. Vasilash, “Flexible Thinking: How Need, Innovation, Teamwork a Whole Bunch of Machining Centers Have Transformed TRW Tillsonburg into a Model of Lean Manufacturing,” Automotive Manufacturing & Production 111, no. 10 (October 1999), pp. 64–65.
‹#›

Organizing for Speed: Time-Based Competition (T B C)
Logistics:
The movement of resources into the organization (inbound) and products from the organization (outbound).
Just-in-time (J I T):
Manufactures in very small lots and delivers to the next stage of the production process just as they are needed.
Concurrent engineering:
A design approach in which all relevant functions cooperate jointly in a maximum effort aimed at producing high-quality products that meet customers’ needs.
‹#›

Management in Action Walmart:An Agile Giant?
Walmart is configuring itself to sell as many products as possible across as many platforms as possible. Recently, it has downsized as it strives to streamline services and its supply chain.
Walmart is shrinking its workforce and cutting back on warehouses while expanding its services in new areas. Do you think this strategy will prove effective? Why or why not?
Walmart is often in the news. Does it seem like its strategy to become more agile is working today? Why or why not?
‹#›

In Review
Discuss why it is critical for organizations to be responsive.
Describe the qualities of an organic organization structure.
Identify strategies and dynamic organizational concepts that can improve an organization’s responsiveness.
Distinguish between centralized and decentralized organizations.
Explain how a firm can be both big and small
Identify ways that firms organize around different types of technology.
‹#›

Accessibility Content: Text Alternatives for Images
‹#›

Exhibit 9.1 Formal and Informal Organization Structures – Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images.
The exhibit consists of two organization charts. One highly formalized, with a rigid hierarchy in which the C E O oversees three vice presidents and each vice president has four managers reporting to them.
The other chart is an informal, organic organization. In this structure, the C E O is found more in the midst of the organization, vice presidents are around the C E O and they might be found relating to subordinates that report to other managers. This structure is more flexible than the other.
Return to parent-slide containing images.
‹#›

Exhibit 9.3 Generic Value Chain – Text Alternative
Return to parent-slide containing images.
A giant arrow is shown pointing to the right, depicting the sequence of actions that add value along the chain and produce the profit margin.
Support activities include the firm’s infrastructure, human resource management, technology development, and procurement.
Primary activities that add value with each step include: research and development, inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing and sales, and service.
Both support activities and primary activities contribute to the margin.
Return to parent-slide containing images.
‹#›
